1.exposition: With the thesis stated — a righteousness of God apart from the law, through faith (Romans 3:21–26) — Paul turns to proof, and to the one name no Jewish reader could wave aside: Abraham. What did the patriarch find? If his works had earned him a verdict, he had ground to boast — but not before God. The decisive text is Genesis 15:6: “Abraham believed God, and it was counted to him for righteousness.” Paul presses the logic with a wage analogy — earned pay is a debt owed, not a gift given — and drives it to its scandal: God justifies the ungodly (vv.4–5). Then he calls a second witness, David, who blesses the man whose sin the Lord will not count (vv.6–8, citing Psalms 32:1–2). Torah and the Writings together settle the case: righteousness is reckoned, not earned.
1.original_language: The chapter's drumbeat is logizomai (“to reckon, count, impute”) — a merchant's term for entering an item on the ledger — struck from Genesis 15:6 (LXX elogisthē) and sounded some eleven times in these verses. In v.4 two commercial words divide grace from merit: the worker's misthos (“wage”) is reckoned not as charis (“grace,” a free gift) but as opheilēma (“what is owed,” a debt). Righteousness credited to faith is a gift entered on the books, never a wage claimed by right.
1.historical_context: Paul's appeal is aimed at a living tradition. Second-Temple Judaism prized the zekhut avot (the “merit of the fathers”), and later rabbis would claim that “Abraham kept the whole Torah before it was given” (Qiddushin 4:14), reading Genesis 15:6 as the reward crowning a life of proven faithfulness — above all the binding of Isaac (Genesis 22). Paul reads the same verse the opposite way: the crediting is the justifying, not its wages. And by joining Abraham (from the Law) to David (from the Writings), he meets the two-witness standard of Jewish argument (Deuteronomy 19:15).
1.christ: The God “who justifies the ungodly” (v.5) is the God whose justice Paul has just shown vindicated at the cross (Romans 3:25–26): he can acquit the guilty because their sin was borne by his Son as their surety. The faith that is “counted for righteousness” is therefore not a meritorious achievement but trust in the one God has provided — and the argument's goal is already in view, the God who raised Jesus (v.24).

verses.note: Paul opens the case with the one name no Jewish reader could dismiss: what did Abraham our forefather find according to the flesh? grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the strategy — he proves justification by faith from the very father of the nation, the supreme example of piety, settling the question from the case his readers most revered. “As pertaining to the flesh” asks what Abraham gained by his own effort or natural descent; the answer, coming next, is nothing to glory in. eastern Chrysostom adds that Paul confirms his doctrine from the patriarch himself, so that no Jew can object.

If Abraham was justified by works, he has ground to boast — but not before God. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson read the boast as a claim to merit, real perhaps before men but silenced at the divine tribunal, where justification by works is excluded (Romans 3:27). reformed Calvin turns it around: had Abraham embraced God's mercy by faith, he brought nothing to glory in but a confession of his own misery.

The clinching text: Abraham believed God, and it was counted to him for righteousness (Genesis 15:6). grammatical-historical Robertson presses the verb elogisthē — set down on the credit side of the ledger; the same word is rendered “counted,” “reckoned,” “imputed.” On what was counted the witnesses divide: Barnes holds the act of believing was reckoned for righteousness, faith the instrument and not a transfer; reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical Ellicott read a forensic imputation — faith counted as righteousness because it lays hold on Christ, whose righteousness is the ground, faith not a merit but the means of receiving it.

To the one who works, the wage is reckoned not as a gift but as a debt. grammatical-historical Robertson, with reformed Calvin, marks the “worker” as one who seeks to earn, claiming his pay as a due; eastern Theodoret and Barnes note the plain principle — a misthos earned is owed, claimable by right, and where merit rules, grace has no room.

The gospel's heart: to the one who does not work but believes on him who justifies the ungodly, his faith is counted for righteousness. grammatical-historical Robertson quotes Denney — “the whole Pauline gospel could be summed up in this one word: God who justifies the ungodly.” Not the righteous but the impious is pardoned. reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley draw the sharp corollary: none seeks this righteousness but the one who feels himself ungodly; holiness follows justification and does not precede it, for to be made holy first would set justification aside.

A second witness is called: David describes the blessedness of the man to whom God credits righteousness apart from works. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the weight of adding David, the most eminent king, to Abraham. reformed Calvin fixes the substance — justification here is the remission of sins, and the works excluded are not merely ceremonies but works of every kind.

Blessed are those whose lawless deeds are forgiven, and whose sins are covered (Psalms 32:1). grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the two-fold mercy: iniquities forgiven, the debt remitted; sins covered, hidden from God's view and remembered no more. The blessedness David sings is the same as the imputed righteousness of the verse before — justification is the forgiveness of sins.

Blessed is the man against whom the Lord will not count his sin. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark this as the negative side of imputation — God declining to charge sin to the account, answering to the reckoning of righteousness (v.6). reformed Calvin observes that David stops short of “in whose spirit is no guile,” since his subject is justification, not sanctification; the blessing is the gratuitous non-imputation of sin. reformed Gill adds the ground: such sins the Lord does not charge because he has charged them on his Son, the sinner's surety, who bore them away.

witnesses.point: Paul confirms justification by faith from the very patriarch the Jews revered, so that no Jew can object; and being saved without achievement is no shame but a brighter glory than works could give.
logizomai sets faith on the credit side of the ledger; citing Denney, “God who justifies the ungodly” is the whole Pauline gospel in a single phrase.
Before God even Abraham had nothing to boast but a confession of misery; justification is the remission of sins, and holiness follows it rather than preceding it.
God does not impute sin to the believer because he imputed it to his Son, the surety, who bore it and took it away.

external.name: Douglas J. Moo
external.point: “The God who justifies the ungodly” is deliberately jarring — the God who in the law forbade a judge to acquit the guilty (Exodus 23:7) now does exactly that, on the ground of grace, with faith as the empty hand that receives rather than a work that earns.

1.application: Every attempt to secure a standing before God by achievement lands in the ledger of debt, where God owes nothing. The gospel offers the other column — righteousness credited as a gift to the empty hand of faith. The question is not whether your account is impressive, but whose righteousness is entered in it.

9.exposition: Paul now presses the logic one step further: does the blessedness of Psalms 32:1–2 fall on the circumcised only, or on the uncircumcised also (v.9)? The answer turns on a date. Genesis 15:6, where Abraham's faith was credited, stands years before circumcision was instituted in Genesis 17. Abraham was justified as an uncircumcised man (v.10). Circumcision came afterward as a seal authenticating the righteousness already received, not as its ground. The result reshapes the whole family of God: Abraham is father of two peoples — all who believe though uncircumcised, and all who, being circumcised, also walk in the footsteps of the faith he had before his circumcision (vv.11–12). He is father of all who believe, not only of ethnic Israel.
9.historical_context: Circumcision was the covenant sign, the visible seal of belonging to God's people (Genesis 17:11), and in a Roman congregation of both Jews and Gentiles — many of them lately returned after Claudius's expulsion of Jews from the city (c. AD 49) — the question of whether Gentiles shared Abraham's blessing was no abstraction but the daily friction of shared tables. Paul argues as his rabbinically trained readers would: the earlier text governs the later. The seal image was familiar from commerce and law — a seal authenticates a document but does not create its contents; so circumcision attests a righteousness already possessed, and cannot be its cause.
9.original_language: Circumcision is named a sēmeion (“sign”) and a sphragis (“seal”) of the righteousness of faith (v.11); a seal confirms and authenticates what is already there — it does not produce it. In v.12 Abraham's true children are those who walk in the ichnesin (“footsteps”) of his faith: sonship is following a path already trodden, not merely bearing a mark in the flesh.

verses.note: Does this blessedness fall on the circumcised only, or on the uncircumcised also? grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note Paul pressing toward the universality of justification by faith, recalling the key fact (v.3) — faith was reckoned to Abraham — to set up the decisive question of timing. puritan Matthew Henry sees the point already implied: the blessing was never tied to the rite.

How then was it reckoned — while he was circumcised, or uncircumcised? Not while circumcised, but while uncircumcised. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson lean on the plain order of the narrative: the crediting of Genesis 15:6 stands years before the covenant of circumcision in Genesis 17. Abraham was justified while still, in effect, a Gentile — so justification cannot depend on the rite.

Circumcision, then, was a sign and seal of the righteousness of faith he already had while uncircumcised, so that he might be father of all who believe without being circumcised. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson stress that a seal authenticates but does not create; eastern Theodoret and reformed Calvin agree the rite sealed and did not produce the righteousness, and that Abraham's fatherhood now reaches every believer, that righteousness might be credited to them also.

And he is father of the circumcised who are not merely circumcised but also walk in the footsteps of his faith. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the sharp qualification: descent and the mark in the flesh are not enough; reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret drive it home — among the Jews too, only those who imitate Abraham's faith are his true children. Faith, not the flesh, makes the child.

witnesses.point: The order of Genesis is decisive — Abraham was justified years before circumcision, which was only a sign and seal of a righteousness already his by faith.
Circumcision sealed but did not produce righteousness; Abraham's fatherhood extends to all who share his faith, circumcised or not.
Circumcision alone never made anyone a child of Abraham; only faith does, so the patriarch fathers believing Jew and believing Gentile alike.

external.name: James D.G. Dunn
external.point: Circumcision had become the badge of Jewish covenant identity; by dating Abraham's justification before it, Paul redefines the rite as a seal confirming a righteousness already given by faith, so that Abraham fathers a family defined by faith rather than by ethnicity.

9.application: Religious markers are meant to point, not to prove. Whatever seals your faith — baptism, membership, a heritage — it authenticates a gift already received; it can never be the ground you stand on. Abraham's welcome of the uncircumcised into his family is the gospel's refusal to let any boundary but faith define the people of God.

13.exposition: The promise that Abraham and his seed would be heir of the world came not through the law but through the righteousness of faith. Paul reasons by exclusion: if those of the law are the heirs, faith is emptied and the promise nullified — for the law produces wrath, and where there is no law there is no transgression (vv.14–15). Therefore the inheritance is of faith, that it might rest on grace and so be guaranteed to all the seed — not to the law-keeper only but to everyone who shares Abraham's faith. He is the father of us all, as it stands written, “I have made you a father of many nations” — in the presence of the God he believed, who gives life to the dead and calls into being the things that are not.
13.historical_context: The phrase “heir of the world” quotes no single Genesis verse; it gathers up the swelling Abrahamic promises — land (Genesis 12:7), a countless seed (Genesis 15:5), and blessing for all nations (Genesis 12:3). Second-Temple writers such as Sirach and Jubilees had already spoken of Abraham as heir of the whole earth, and Paul takes up that universalism while re-grounding it — not in Abraham's merits but in the God who calls into being what is not. His description of that God as one who “gives life to the dead” (v.17) echoes the daily synagogue prayer, the second benediction of the Amidah, which praised the God who revives the dead.
13.original_language: The argument pivots on two opposed words: epangelia (“promise,” the free pledge) against nomos (“law,” the demand that must be met). The inheritance belongs to a klēronomos (“heir”) by promise, not as a wage. And the God Abraham trusts “calls the things that are not as though they were” — kalountos ta mē onta hōs onta (v.17) — the language of creation out of nothing, the same power that will raise the dead.

verses.note: The promise that Abraham and his seed would be heir of the world came not through the law but through the righteousness of faith. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson read the vast promise as fulfilled in the worldwide family of faith and its blessing through Christ. puritan Matthew Henry and reformed Calvin note the decisive chronology: the promise stood centuries before the law was given, and so cannot rest upon it.

If those of the law are the heirs, faith is emptied and the promise nullified. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson state the alternative starkly — make law-keeping the ground, and faith is made void, the free promise destroyed. reformed Calvin presses the principle: law and faith cannot both be grounds of justification; to admit the one is to abolish the other.

The reason follows: the law works wrath, and where there is no law there is no transgression. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note that the law, broken by all, can only condemn — it exposes guilt and brings the curse, never the inheritance. reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley agree the law produces wrath precisely because none keep it; and by defining sin it turns it into transgression, the formal violation of a known command.

Therefore the whole thing is of faith, that it might rest on grace, and so the promise be guaranteed to all the seed. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson, with reformed Calvin, note that faith and grace belong together as law and debt do — a promise resting on performance is only as sure as its weakest keeper, but one resting on grace is certain to all. eastern Theodoret marks the reach: Abraham is the father of us all, Jew and Gentile.

As it is written, “I have made you a father of many nations” (Genesis 17:5) — before the God he believed, who gives life to the dead and calls into being the things that are not. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the object of Abraham's faith: the God of resurrection, who would raise a seed from his own and Sarah's “dead” bodies. puritan Matthew Henry and reformed Calvin add the Creator's power — calling into existence what does not yet exist — the same power that would raise Jesus from the dead (v.24).

witnesses.point: The promise of a worldwide inheritance came by faith and stood centuries before the law; the law can only work wrath, never the inheritance.
Law and faith cannot both be the ground; the inheritance is of faith so that it may rest on grace, and the promise be sure to all the seed.
Abraham believed the God who quickens the dead and calls the things that are not as though they were — faith resting wholly on omnipotence.

external.name: N.T. Wright
external.point: “Heir of the world” stretches the Abrahamic promise past the land of Canaan to the whole renewed creation; the single family of faith, Jew and Gentile, inherits the cosmos God means to remake — the inheritance the resurrection sets in motion.

13.application: A promise you must earn is only ever as secure as your last performance. Paul's whole point is that God grounded the inheritance in grace precisely so that it would be certain — to the strong and the weak, the impressive and the broken, alike. Rest your assurance there, not on the fluctuating record of your obedience.

18.exposition: Paul now unfolds what that faith actually was. Against hope, in hope Abraham believed, on the bare word “So shall your seed be” (Genesis 15:5). Not weak in faith, he faced the deadness of his own body and Sarah's womb (v.19), yet did not waver through unbelief but grew strong in faith, giving glory to God, fully persuaded that God could do what he had promised. That is why “it was counted to him for righteousness.” And those words, Paul insists, were written not for Abraham alone but for us who believe on him who raised Jesus our Lord from the dead — delivered for our trespasses and raised for our justification. The case that opened with Abraham's crediting closes at the empty tomb.
18.christ: Here the whole argument reaches its goal. The God Abraham trusted was named “the one who gives life to the dead” (v.17); the God the believer trusts “raised Jesus our Lord from the dead” (v.24) — the faith-pattern is identical, trust in the God whose power overrides impossibility. The compact creed of v.25 names both pillars: “delivered for our offences” (the atonement, in the language of the Servant, Isaiah 53:5) and “raised for our justification” (the resurrection its proof and seal, 1 Corinthians 15:17). The raising is God's public verdict that the cross has been accepted.
18.original_language: Two words fix the quality of Abraham's faith: in v.20 he “did not waver” (ou diekrithē, was not divided against himself) through unbelief, and in v.21 he was “fully persuaded” (plērophorētheis, carried to complete conviction). The creed of v.25 then balances two passive verbs on identical grammar — paredothē (“he was handed over”) for our trespasses and ēgerthē (“he was raised”) for our justification — the parallel binding cross and resurrection into one saving act.
18.historical_context: The promise of a seed like the stars (Genesis 15:5) was spoken into barrenness, and Isaac was not born until Genesis 21 — the faith Paul describes is not a burst of optimism but perseverance across decades of waiting. Abraham's clear-eyed reckoning of his own and Sarah's “dead” bodies is faith's exercise, not its failure. And the two-clause summary of v.25 carries the cadence of Isaiah's Servant, “handed over” for the sins of many (Isaiah 53:5), which the synagogue's readers of Isaiah would have caught.

verses.note: Against hope, in hope he believed, that he might become father of many nations, as he had been told, “So shall your seed be” (Genesis 15:5). grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson catch the paradox — every natural hope of offspring was gone, yet Abraham hoped on the bare promise. eastern Theodoret and reformed Calvin note that his faith rose above natural reason, resting wholly on the word of God.

Not weak in faith, he faced the fact that his own body was as good as dead, being about a hundred years old, and the deadness of Sarah's womb. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson stress that his confidence was not shaken though he weighed the impossibilities honestly. reformed Calvin draws the lesson: true faith does not shut its eyes to difficulty but rises above it, resting on the power of God.

He did not waver at the promise of God through unbelief, but grew strong in faith, giving glory to God. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the steadiness — no hesitation, no division of mind. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret name the fruit: faith glorifies God by honoring his power and truth; to trust the promise is to render him the worship due his omnipotence.

He was fully persuaded that what God had promised he was able also to perform. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark the settled, entire conviction — not a wavering hope but complete assurance. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret locate here the very essence of faith: trusting not the visible probabilities but the power and faithfulness of God to keep his word.

That is why “it was counted to him for righteousness.” grammatical-historical Barnes and reformed Gill draw the inference: because of this strong, God-glorifying faith, the crediting of v.3 (Genesis 15:6) was made. reformed Calvin adds the qualification that guards the whole chapter — it was counted not as a meritorious work but as the hand laying hold on God's promise.

The words “it was counted to him” were not written for his sake alone. eastern Chrysostom, with grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson, notes that the record of Abraham's justification is no mere history about one man but instruction for every believer. puritan Matthew Henry hears the comfort: God's dealings with Abraham were written for our learning, that we might know this justification is ours too.

They were written for us also, to whom it will be counted, who believe on him who raised Jesus our Lord from the dead. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note that righteousness is credited to us on the very terms it was to Abraham — by believing. eastern Cyril and reformed Calvin draw the parallel that crowns the chapter: as Abraham trusted the God who gives life to the dead (v.17), so we trust the God who raised Christ; the two faiths share one object, the life-giving God.

He was delivered up for our trespasses and raised for our justification. grammatical-historical Barnes, with reformed Gill and wesleyan-arminian Clarke, reads the first clause of the atonement — Christ handed to death to satisfy for our sins (Isaiah 53:5); Barnes and grammatical-historical Ellicott read the second of the resurrection as the atonement's proof and seal (1 Corinthians 15:17). Here the witnesses divide: wesleyan-arminian Clarke insists we are saved by Christ's death and not by an imputed obedience to the moral law, while reformed Gill holds Christ's whole righteousness, obedience included, reckoned to the believer.

witnesses.point: Abraham hoped against hope, fully persuaded God could perform; righteousness is credited to us on the same terms — by believing on the God who raised Jesus.
Faith does not ignore difficulty but rises above it, glorifying God by trusting his power; it was counted for righteousness not as a work but as laying hold on the promise.
It is by Christ's death for our offences, not by an imputed active obedience to the moral law, that the believer is justified.
Christ's whole righteousness, his obedience included, is imputed to the believer, and his resurrection seals an atonement God has accepted.

external.name: C.E.B. Cranfield
external.point: The two balanced clauses of v.25 should not be split into airtight compartments; the resurrection is bound up with our justification as the cross is with our sins — the raising of Jesus is God's public declaration that the atonement has been accepted and the verdict secured.

18.application: Abraham's faith and ours are the same faith, aimed at the same God — the one who brings life out of death. His was tried across decades of barrenness; ours rests on an empty tomb. When the promise seems contradicted by everything you can see, this is the pattern: not to deny the facts, but to give glory to God as able to do what he has said.

_meta.title: The Faith of Abraham, Father of Us All
reflection: God “justifies the ungodly” and credits faith, not works, as righteousness (v.5). Where are you still trying to earn a standing before God that can only be received as a gift?

Circumcision came to Abraham as a seal of a righteousness he already had by faith, years before the rite (vv.10–11). What religious markers or achievements do you quietly treat as the ground of your acceptance rather than the sign of it?

Abraham “against hope believed in hope” and “gave glory to God,” fully persuaded God could do what he promised (v.18, vv.20–21) — and our faith rests where his did, on the God who “raised Jesus our Lord” (v.24). Where is God asking you to trust his power against the evidence of your circumstances?
